title: Staining and Imaging of Mouse Submandibular Ganglion by α-Bungarotoxin and Nanosensor
author: Hongrong Yang
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.wxrffm6

steps.title: Rinse submandibular ganglion with PBS for 00:20:00
steps.instruction: Rinse submandibular ganglion with PBS for 00:20:00

steps.title: Soak submandibular ganglion in 500 µL 0
steps.instruction: Soak submandibular ganglion in 500 µL 0.000002 Molarity (M) α-Bungarotoxin Alexa Fluor 647 conjugated Thermo Fisher Scientific Catalog #B35450 for 00:30:00 . Alternatively, soak submandibular ganglion in 500 µL nanosensor solution formulated by Clark Lab for 00:30:00

steps.title: Rinse submandibular ganglion with PBS for 00:20:00
steps.instruction: Rinse submandibular ganglion with PBS for 00:20:00 .

1. Place the submandibular ganglion on a glass slide and cover it with cover slip. 2. Image the whole tissue using a Zeiss LSM 700 confocal. Use 488 nm laser line for GFP and Oregan Green 488 channel, 555 nm laser line for pHAb, and 639 nm laser line for bungarotoxin-Alexa Fluor 647 channel. Set laser power at 2% or at the appropriate power intensity that cellular structures can be clearly seen. ZEN pro ZEISS

steps.title: Make a midline incision in the...
steps.instruction: Make a midline incision in the neck and expose the salivary glands.

steps.title: Separate the pair of salivary glands...
steps.instruction: Separate the pair of salivary glands and expose the salivary ducts.

steps.title: Remove connective tissues surrounding salivary ducts...
steps.instruction: Remove connective tissues surrounding salivary ducts and cut the ducts from where they enter salivary glands to where they enter digastric muscles. The submandibular ganglion are attached to cutted ducts. Well isolated salivary ducts with at least one submandibular ganglion attached
